candidate_problem: Decoupled task allocation and routing in multi-agent systems leads to local optima and ignores the coupled nature of the objective function.
evidence_spans: heuristic approaches offer faster solutions but are prone to getting trapped in local optima [ 9 ] . This is largely because they tend to decouple the problem (i.e., solving allocation and routing sequentially) thereby ignoring the coupled nature of the objective function [ 25 ] .
problem_evidence_spans: Existing learning-based frameworks exhibit various limitations when applied to the multi-stage map coverage problem. The majority of current literature focuses either on decentralized architectures... or on “flat” encoding schemes that fail to capture the hierarchical dependencies
feasibility_evidence_spans: The proposed architecture autoregressively generates a solution by decoding the hierarchical structure of the problem in a single pass.
The proposed method achieves a speedup of four orders of magnitude over traditional exact solvers.
auto_research_experiment: Train an ARMATA-style autoregressive transformer on the Multi-Agent Traveling Salesman Problem (mTSP) using synthetic coordinate data. Compare the joint decoding strategy against a decoupled baseline where tasks are first clustered (K-Means) and then routed (LKH-3).
available_data_or_benchmark: Google OR-Tools (Routing Library) and LKH-3 solver for generating baselines on synthetic spatial distributions.
public_training_or_eval_data: https://github.com/google/or-tools
scientific_hypothesis: A centralized autoregressive model that decodes assignment and routing tokens in a single sequence will achieve lower total path costs than a two-stage pipeline because it can account for routing costs during the assignment phase.
expected_metric: Total Path Cost (Sum of distances)
success_threshold: 15% reduction in total path cost compared to the decoupled K-Means + LKH-3 baseline.
failure_condition: The model fails to converge or produces invalid tours (e.g., missing nodes or duplicate visits) that require more post-processing than the heuristic baseline.
first_24h_experiment: Generate 10,000 synthetic mTSP instances with 20 nodes and 3 agents. Implement a basic Pointer Network with a multi-token output (agent_id, node_id) and measure the initial loss convergence.
specific_intervention: Implement a multi-stage decoding mechanism where the model predicts the next node and the assigned agent ID simultaneously in an autoregressive sequence.
baseline: Decoupled pipeline: K-Means clustering for assignment followed by LKH-3 for routing.
story_angle: By treating multi-agent logistics as a single autoregressive sequence rather than two separate math problems, we can solve NP-hard routing tasks in milliseconds instead of hours.

candidate_problem: The state-of-the-art Adam optimizer is evolutionarily unfaithful because its additive momentum term allows for deterministic movement in the absence of selection pressure, which is an evolutionary impossibility.
evidence_spans: A notable outlier, however, is the state-of-the-art Adam optimizer. In order to be brought into strict evolutionary compliance, Adam’s momentum term requires some minor but principled mathematical surgery.
A notable outlier is Adam, whose inertial momentum term permits deterministic movement in the absence of selection pressure — an evolutionary impossibility.
problem_evidence_spans: A notable outlier is Adam, whose inertial momentum term permits deterministic movement in the absence of selection pressure — an evolutionary impossibility.
feasibility_evidence_spans: Code available at https://github.com/danielgrimmer/adam-dls
As the Rosenbrock benchmark (Fig. 5 ) confirms, this surgery preserves Adam’s capacity to navigate ill-conditioned landscapes.
auto_research_experiment: Compare the evolutionary fidelity of standard Adam versus 'Surgical Adam' (DLS-compliant) by measuring the deviation from the Price equation in a population-based simulation across various selection pressures.
available_data_or_benchmark: Rosenbrock function, Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) benchmarks.
public_training_or_eval_data: https://github.com/danielgrimmer/adam-dls
scientific_hypothesis: Replacing Adam's additive momentum with a rank-1 projection (DLS surgery) maintains optimization performance on ill-conditioned landscapes while satisfying the DLS noise relation for evolutionary fidelity.
expected_metric: Optimization convergence speed and 'Evolutionary Fidelity Score' (adherence to the Price equation).
success_threshold: Surgical Adam achieves <10% deviation from the Price equation while maintaining convergence within 1.5x of standard Adam on the Rosenbrock benchmark.
failure_condition: Surgical Adam fails to converge on ill-conditioned PINN landscapes where standard Adam succeeds.
first_24h_experiment: Clone the provided repository, run the Rosenbrock benchmark (Fig 5) comparing standard Adam and the DLS-modified Adam, and log the gradient alignment over time.
specific_intervention: Implement the 'minor mathematical surgery' on Adam's momentum: replace additive momentum with a rank-1 projection that amplifies selection pressure only along historically successful directions.
baseline: Standard Adam optimizer (Kingma & Ba, 2014).
metric: Convergence rate (loss vs. iterations) and DLS noise relation compliance.
story_angle: Fixing the 'evolutionary impossibility' in our most popular AI optimizer to turn engineering tools into scientific simulators.

feasibility_evidence_spans: future work incorporating pathway-based or supervised feature selection methods may improve biological interpretability.
auto_research_experiment: Compare Variance-based selection against Pathway-based selection (using MSigDB Hallmark gene sets) and Supervised selection (Recursive Feature Elimination) using Logistic Regression on TCGA-BRCA data.

success_threshold: A 15% relative increase in F1 score for the BRCA_Normal subtype.
failure_condition: No improvement in minority class F1 score or a decrease in overall Macro F1 score.

candidate_problem: Existing evolutionary-gradient bridges like GO-EA are limited to isotropic approximations (SGD) and cannot produce advanced pre-conditioned algorithms like Natural Gradient Descent or quasi-Newton methods.
evidence_spans: because the GO-EA algorithmic framework relies on random sampling and a greedy search to approximate the gradient, its updates remain effectively isotropic. By contrast, it follows directly from the Price equation, Eq. ( 3 ), that the population’s variance naturally acts as an anisotropic pre-conditioner.
problem_evidence_spans: it is structurally unable to produce a wider family of pre-conditioned gradient algorithms (such as Adam, Natural Gradient Descent, or quasi-Newton methods).
feasibility_evidence_spans: By defining V g via L-BFGS or quasi-Newton pre-conditioning and coupling it with the DLS noise relation, an evolutionary simulation is now available that brings exactly the anisotropic variance these ill-conditioned landscapes demand.
auto_research_experiment: Implement a DLS simulation where the variance schedule is determined by the L-BFGS Hessian approximation and verify it outperforms isotropic GO-EA on stiff PINN loss landscapes.
available_data_or_benchmark: Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) loss landscapes (Rathore et al., 2024).
public_training_or_eval_data: https://github.com/danielgrimmer/adam-dls
scientific_hypothesis: Anisotropic DLS simulations using quasi-Newton pre-conditioning (L-BFGS) will converge significantly faster than isotropic evolutionary algorithms on ill-conditioned fitness landscapes.
expected_metric: Condition number reduction and convergence iterations.
success_threshold: Reduction of the condition number of the Hessian by a factor of 1000x compared to isotropic methods.
failure_condition: The DLS noise relation becomes computationally intractable when coupled with full-rank pre-conditioners.
first_24h_experiment: Modify the DLS codebase to use an L-BFGS pre-conditioner instead of a diagonal one and test on a 2D ill-conditioned quadratic bowl.
specific_intervention: Define the population variance schedule V_g using L-BFGS pre-conditioning and couple it with the DLS noise relation.
baseline: Isotropic GO-EA (Gillespie-Orr Evolutionary Algorithm).
story_angle: Unlocking the power of Newton's method for evolutionary biology by treating population variance as a pre-conditioner.

candidate_problem: Inability of standard monotone response functions (e.g., Hill functions) to parsimoniously model non-monotone biological responses like overshoot and adaptation.
evidence_spans: The well-known Hill functions remain an appropriate null model for monotone saturating responses... Its purpose is to provide an alternative when the observed response is nonmonotone, adaptive, or shaped by competing positive and negative processes
Standard saturating primitives, e.g. the Hill function, are monotone and require at least a difference of two such blocks to produce the same shape.
problem_evidence_spans: In such cases, a monotone response function is insufficient, whereas our EML module, Eq. 2, supports an internal optimum with a small number of parameters.
feasibility_evidence_spans: The Nanda et al. analysis (Fig. 2, Fig. 3) makes this concrete: at depth one.
AIC and BIC both select K = 6 as the optimal cascade depth (Table S4), one depth below the held-out wMSE plateau
auto_research_experiment: Compare the EML-ODE grammar against a standard Hill-function-difference baseline on a diverse set of non-monotone time-series data from the BioModels database to determine if EML consistently achieves lower AIC/BIC at low depth.
available_data_or_benchmark: Rho-GTPase perturbation data from Nanda et al. (2023) and PKA regulatory-subunit relocalization data from LaCroix et al. (2022).
public_training_or_eval_data: https://github.com/paultlandon/eml-biological-dynamics (implied repository for paper code/data)
scientific_hypothesis: An EML-based operator (exp(x) - ln(y)) provides a more parsimonious (fewer parameters) representation of biphasic biological responses than a linear combination of monotone Hill functions.
expected_metric: AIC (Akaike Information Criterion)
success_threshold: EML-ODE models achieve lower AIC than Hill-difference models in >75% of non-monotone biological time-series samples.
failure_condition: Hill-difference models achieve equal or lower AIC with the same or fewer parameters.
first_24h_experiment: Download the Nanda et al. Rho-GTPase dataset, implement the EML depth-1 module and a difference-of-two-Hills model, and compare their AIC on the four reported response traces.
specific_intervention: Replace the standard Hill function primitive with the EML-generated module R^alpha - beta*R in the ODE target function.
baseline: A difference of two Hill functions (H1(R) - H2(R)) representing competing activation and inhibition.
metric: Weighted Mean Squared Error (wMSE) on held-out time points.
story_angle: Biological systems often 'overshoot' or adapt, yet our mathematical tools for modeling them are often either too simple or too complex. A new operator, EML, provides a 'Goldilocks' grammar for discovering the hidden logic of these dynamics.

candidate_problem: The reconstruction-fidelity gap in SSL-based speech representations due to the loss of high-frequency acoustic cues during discriminative training.
evidence_spans: SSL encoders are trained with discriminative objectives that intentionally discard high-frequency, phase-sensitive acoustic cues indispensable for high-fidelity speech synthesis.
Naively decoding SSL features therefore yields perceptibly degraded speech.
problem_evidence_spans: Naively decoding SSL features therefore yields perceptibly degraded speech.
feasibility_evidence_spans: Stage 2 unfreezes the SSL encoder and jointly optimizes the entire pipeline with an end-to-end speech reconstruction objective
semantic anchoring regularizer strictly tethers both the fine-tuned encoder features and the auto-encoder output to the frozen SSL reference

Compare two methods: 1) Standard fine-tuning for reconstruction (MSE loss only), and 2) WavCube's Stage 2 (Reconstruction loss + Semantic Anchoring loss). Evaluate on the SUPERB benchmark to see which method better preserves original understanding performance while improving Mel-cepstral distortion (MCD).
available_data_or_benchmark: LibriTTS (for high-fidelity reconstruction), SUPERB (for semantic preservation).
public_training_or_eval_data: http://www.openslr.org/60/
scientific_hypothesis: A semantic anchoring loss is necessary during acoustic fine-tuning of SSL models to prevent 'catastrophic forgetting' of the semantic manifold while gaining acoustic reconstruction fidelity.
expected_metric: SUPERB Score (Average across tasks)
success_threshold: Maintain >95% of the original WavLM-Large SUPERB score while reducing MCD by at least 1.5 dB.
failure_condition: SUPERB score drops by >10%, indicating the 'acoustic enrichment' destroyed the semantic utility of the representation.
first_24h_experiment: Fine-tune the last 2 layers of WavLM-Large on 10 hours of LibriTTS with a simple linear decoder and measure the change in Phoneme Recognition (PR) accuracy on the SUPERB dev set.
specific_intervention: Jointly optimize the SSL encoder and an acoustic decoder using a weighted sum of reconstruction loss and a semantic anchoring loss (KL divergence or MSE against frozen SSL features).
baseline: Frozen SSL encoder with a trained acoustic decoder (Stage 1 only).
story_angle: Bridging the gap between 'understanding' and 'hearing': how to teach a semantic AI model to care about high-fidelity sound without losing its mind.

candidate_problem: The 'universality risk' of EML-generated symbolic regression leading to overfitting without a restricted grammar.
evidence_spans: The universality of EML is a risk as well as a strength. Without a restricted grammar and explicit penalties, EML can generate arbitrary curve-fitting expressions.
problem_evidence_spans: Otherwise, a sufficiently expressive symbolic system becomes a language for overfitting.
feasibility_evidence_spans: Model selection used the held-out score S(E) = ... + lambda_d * d(E) + lambda_n * n(E), where d(E) is EML depth and n(E) is node count.
auto_research_experiment: Systematically vary the penalty coefficients (lambda_d, lambda_n) in the EML search grammar across varying noise levels in the toy 50-state activation-adaptation network to find the optimal regularization path.
available_data_or_benchmark: The 50-state activation-adaptation network simulation described in the Appendix.
public_training_or_eval_data: Synthetic data generator based on Eqs. S28-S33.
scientific_hypothesis: A depth-proportional penalty (lambda_d) is more effective than a node-count penalty (lambda_n) at preventing overfitting in EML-based dynamical model discovery.
expected_metric: Generalization Error (Held-out wMSE)
success_threshold: Depth-penalized models show significantly lower variance in held-out error across different noise realizations compared to node-penalized models.
failure_condition: Node-count penalties or no penalties perform equally well in preventing overfitting.
first_24h_experiment: Replicate the toy benchmark simulation (Eqs. S28-S33) and perform a grid search over lambda_d and lambda_n to map the Pareto front of complexity vs. error.
specific_intervention: Varying the structural penalty terms in the model selection objective function.
baseline: Unpenalized EML-ODE search (lambda_d = lambda_n = 0).
story_angle: To prevent 'mathematical hallucinations' in biological modeling, researchers are testing new structural penalties that favor dynamical depth over raw complexity.

candidate_problem: The paper identifies a gap in mapping specific multi-agent coordination architectures to predictable failure modes (MAST taxonomy), specifically noting that sequential pipelines are prone to task-verification gaps (FM-3) due to lack of architectural verification opportunities.
evidence_spans: The MAST taxonomy of Cemri et al. ( 2025 ) identifies three top-level failure clusters: system-design issues, inter-agent misalignment, and task-verification gaps.
a configuration with strict stage ordering and no architectural opportunity for upstream verification (sequential pipeline) is a plausible breeding ground for FM-3.

Predicting which of these MAST failure modes a particular configuration is most likely to exhibit from the configuration’s specification alone —the goal articulated in Section 2.1 —appears feasible at the resolution of this experiment.
problem_evidence_spans: a configuration with strict stage ordering and no architectural opportunity for upstream verification (sequential pipeline) is a plausible breeding ground for FM-3.

auto_research_experiment: Using the provided 'coordination-experiment' harness, scale the evaluation of the 'sequential pipeline' and 'consensus alignment' architectures from n=100 to n=500 markets. Specifically, measure the frequency of FM-3 (task-verification gaps) in the sequential pipeline versus the baseline consensus model using the Murphy decomposition and trace analysis.
available_data_or_benchmark: Polymarket binary markets and the Foresight Arena live agent harness.
public_training_or_eval_data: https://github.com/ForesightFlow/datasets/tree/main/coordination-traces-100
scientific_hypothesis: Increasing the sample size to n=500 will statistically confirm that sequential pipeline architectures exhibit a significantly higher rate of FM-3 (task-verification) failures compared to architectures with built-in verification layers.
expected_metric: Frequency of FM-3 failure signatures in execution traces.
success_threshold: Statistically significant difference (p < 0.05 after Bonferroni correction) in FM-3 rates between sequential and consensus architectures.
failure_condition: The FM-3 failure rate does not significantly differ between architectures even at n=500, suggesting the failure is model-bound rather than architecture-bound.
first_24h_experiment: Clone the repository, verify the harness runs on a subset of 10 new Polymarket IDs, and confirm the trace-logger correctly flags 'inadequate output checking' (FM-3).
specific_intervention: Scaling the sample size to n=500 and isolating the 'sequential pipeline' architecture to measure FM-3 frequency.
baseline: The 'consensus alignment' architecture (reported as having lower FM-3 risk but higher FM-2 risk).
story_angle: Moving from 'engineering convenience' to 'architectural science' by proving that how we connect agents deterministically creates specific failure types.

candidate_problem: Spurious divergence in image-derived glymphatic velocity fields violating conservation of mass
evidence_spans: Measurement and reconstruction errors in image-derived velocity fields commonly manifest as spurious divergence of the velocity field, leading to the violation of the conservation of mass.
reconstructed velocity fields frequently reflect experimental artifacts rather than the underlying physics.
problem_evidence_spans: Measurement and reconstruction errors in image-derived velocity fields commonly manifest as spurious divergence of the velocity field, leading to the violation of the conservation of mass.
feasibility_evidence_spans: In preclinical mouse brain models—the focus of the present work—glymphatic transport is typically captured via contrast-enhanced MRI (CE-MRI)
The resulting modified advection-diffusion equation is solved using an immersed finite element method within an isogeometric analysis (IGA) framework.
auto_research_experiment: Implement the Hughes-Wells (HW) additive decomposition as a physics-based regularizer within a Newton-based inverse solver to recover solenoidal velocity fields from noisy CE-MRI tracer data.
available_data_or_benchmark: Preclinical contrast-enhanced MRI (CE-MRI) time-series of tracer transport in mouse brains.
public_training_or_eval_data: The 'Glymphatic MRI' dataset or similar open-access preclinical neuroimaging repositories (e.g., ADNI or specialized mouse glymphatic datasets like those from Mestre et al. 2018).
scientific_hypothesis: Enforcing a divergence-free constraint via the Hughes-Wells formulation in an inverse setting will significantly reduce tracer conservation errors compared to unconstrained finite-difference velocity extraction.
expected_metric: Conservation error (L2 norm of the divergence of the velocity field) and tracer concentration RMSE.
success_threshold: Divergence of the reconstructed field < 1e-6 and tracer mass conservation error < 1% over the simulation duration.
failure_condition: The Newton solver fails to converge or the corrective velocity component dominates the physical signal, leading to non-physiological transport patterns.
first_24h_experiment: Generate a synthetic 3D tracer transport dataset with known solenoidal velocity and added Gaussian noise; attempt to recover the velocity field using the HW-modified inverse solver vs. a standard unconstrained solver.
specific_intervention: Hughes-Wells (HW) additive velocity decomposition (u = u_bar + grad(phi)) applied as a regularizer in the inverse advection-diffusion problem.
baseline: Standard unconstrained inverse reconstruction or direct finite-difference estimation from voxelized intensity changes.
story_angle: By treating the brain's waste-clearance system as a fluid dynamics problem with strict conservation laws, we can finally see the 'invisible' currents that flush out Alzheimer's-linked proteins.

feasibility_evidence_spans: IGA facilitates the projection of these high-dimensional image data and transport fields onto B-spline basis functions, naturally providing regularization and smoothing.
auto_research_experiment: Compare B-spline basis function order (p=2 vs p=4) in an IGA framework to determine the minimum resolution required to preserve local velocity peaks in the perivascular space (PVS) without increasing noise sensitivity.

scientific_hypothesis: Higher-order B-spline basis functions in an immersed IGA framework can recover sub-voxel velocity peaks in perivascular spaces that are lost in standard voxel-based finite element methods.
expected_metric: Peak Velocity Recovery Ratio (PVRR) compared to ground truth high-res simulations.
success_threshold: Recovery of >80% of local velocity peaks within the PVS at standard MRI resolutions (approx 100um).
failure_condition: High-order splines introduce oscillatory artifacts (Runge's phenomenon) that destabilize the advection-diffusion solver.
first_24h_experiment: Create a 1D synthetic signal with sharp peaks representing PVS flow; compare B-spline interpolation vs. linear interpolation in the presence of 5% noise.
specific_intervention: High-order B-spline basis functions (p >= 3) in an Isogeometric Analysis (IGA) framework.
baseline: Standard linear Finite Element Method (FEM) on a voxel-conforming mesh.
story_angle: Standard brain scans are too blurry to see the tiny 'pipes' that clean the brain; new mathematical 'lenses' using B-splines might sharpen our view without needing better hardware.

candidate_problem: The instability of symbolic coding thresholds in multi-channel signaling games leads to 'strategic collapse' where agents fail to stabilize a shared code.
evidence_spans: coding innovations may be vulnerable to strategic collapse due to multichannel failures, thus increasing the risk of systemic fragility and evolutionary dead ends.

Our game-theoretic analysis is intended to shift the interpretation of “hard steps” from elusive improbability to equilibrium structure, arguing that algorithmic bottlenecks correspond to unstable equilibria where signaling, coordination, and control must tightly align.
problem_evidence_spans: defection — whether by minimizing signaling effort or exploiting shared resources — is tempting.
These dynamics must not only stabilize themselves, but also invent new information architectures that unlock further evolutionary phase spaces
feasibility_evidence_spans: The emergence of technological society can be modeled as an iterated or evolutionary n-player Prisoner’s Dilemma (Commons Dilemma)

auto_research_experiment: Simulate an evolutionary multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) environment where agents must develop a shared symbolic mapping (a 'code') to solve a coordination task. Introduce 'signaling cost' and 'multi-channel noise'. Test if the population collapses into a non-communicative state (stagnation) when the complexity of the task requires a 'coding threshold' (mapping discrete symbols to abstract functions) versus simple indexical signaling.
available_data_or_benchmark: The 'Emergent Communication' (EmerComms) benchmarks in MARL, specifically the 'Lewis Signaling Games' or 'Referential Games' frameworks.
public_training_or_eval_data: EGG (Emergence of Language in Games) toolkit by Facebook Research.
scientific_hypothesis: The transition from indexical signaling to symbolic coding is an unstable equilibrium; increasing the number of signaling channels or the cost of high-fidelity translation will cause a population to revert to low-complexity signaling (the 'Great Filter' bottleneck) unless a specific 'JOOTS' (Jump Out Of The System) mechanism is present.
expected_metric: Code Stability Index (CSI) and Task Success Rate.
success_threshold: CSI > 0.8 in high-noise environments with symbolic mapping.
failure_condition: Agents converge to a 'babbling' equilibrium where symbols have no consistent functional mapping (CSI < 0.2).
first_24h_experiment: Set up a standard Lewis Game using the EGG toolkit. Introduce a 'mutation' rate in the symbol-to-meaning mapping and a 'metabolic cost' for using more than 5 distinct symbols. Measure the population's ability to maintain a 10-symbol lexicon over 1000 generations.
specific_intervention: Introduce a 'translation machinery' cost (simulating the ribosome or grammar) that must be paid collectively to unlock higher-order rewards.
baseline: Standard Sender-Receiver game without symbolic mapping costs (purely indexical).
metric: Topographic Similarity (correlation between symbol distance and meaning distance).
story_angle: The 'Great Filter' might not be a lack of life, but a mathematical trap where complex communication systems collapse under their own strategic weight before they can stabilize.

success_threshold: Identify a statistically significant correlation (p < 0.01) between tracking precision and goal-inference speed across 1000+ simulated trajectories.

first_24h_experiment: Implement a 2D point-mass simulation with a Lyapunov-based tracking controller and a Rao-Blackwellized Particle Filter observer. Log the KL-divergence of the observer's belief as the tracking gain is increased.

baseline: Standard Lyapunov-based tracking controller (no obfuscation) and the Dragan et al. (2013) legibility-optimized controller.
metric: KL-divergence between adversary belief and true goal location.
story_angle: As autonomous systems become more predictable to observers, we must find the mathematical limit where 'being safe' and 'being private' become mutually exclusive.

candidate_problem: First-order isotropic evolutionary algorithms (like GO-EA or basic SGA) struggle to navigate the ill-conditioned, stiff loss landscapes of Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs).
evidence_spans: Because PINNs embed partial differential equations directly into the loss function, the resulting loss landscape is notoriously ill-conditioned and riddled with degenerate saddle points... First-order isotropic methods (like basic SGA) or even diagonal methods (like RMSProp) struggle profoundly in such stiff landscapes.

By defining V g via L-BFGS or quasi-Newton pre-conditioning and coupling it with the DLS noise relation, an evolutionary simulation is now available that brings exactly the anisotropic variance these ill-conditioned landscapes demand.
problem_evidence_spans: First-order isotropic methods (like basic SGA) or even diagonal methods (like RMSProp) struggle profoundly in such stiff landscapes because the simulated dynamics cannot reliably follow the sharp fitness ridges
feasibility_evidence_spans: utilizing quasi-Newton pre-conditioning (e.g., L-BFGS) has been empirically proven to reduce the magnitude of the eigenvalues and the condition number of the PINN Hessian by a factor of 1000 or more

story_angle: Using evolutionary first principles to solve the 'stiffness' problem in Physics-Informed Neural Networks.

candidate_problem: Euclidean distance-based connectivity in dynamic occupancy models fails to account for landscape resistance and barriers, leading to biased environmental inference for species like the Eurasian otter.
evidence_spans: Integrating a resistance-based formulation of connectivity would be particularly relevant for species like the otter which primarily disperse along rivers and coastlines
Euclidean distance is often replaced in connectivity analyses by functional metrics like least-cost path

environmental effects appeared comparatively moderate in our case (Fig. 4), with a high and relatively homogeneous installation probability across the country. This suggests that, at least in France, part of the observed association between otter presence and environmental variables arises from dispersal history rather than habitat selection.
problem_evidence_spans: The model can be improved by incorporating more functional definitions of connectivity. Indeed, we defined connectivity as a function of Euclidean distance between sites.
dispersal is often constrained by anthropogenic or natural barriers that Euclidean distance alone cannot capture.
feasibility_evidence_spans: we introduce a dispersal-pressure formulation that provides a flexible and ecologically interpretable representation of the colonization process, and develop a sparse distance matrix implementation that enables application to large study areas comprising several thousand sites.
auto_research_experiment: Replace the Euclidean distance matrix in the paper's sparse implementation with a Least-Cost Path (LCP) distance matrix derived from a river-network resistance surface for the Eurasian otter (Lutra lutra) dataset. Compare the model fit (WAIC) and the magnitude of environmental coefficients (e.g., cropland, riparian vegetation) between the Euclidean and LCP versions.
available_data_or_benchmark: The paper uses French national monitoring data for Eurasian otters (Lutra lutra) and Grey wolves (Canis lupus).
public_training_or_eval_data: The French National Biodiversity Observatory (ONB) and the French Biodiversity Agency (OFB) provide species occurrence data; environmental layers (Corine Land Cover) are public.
scientific_hypothesis: Incorporating river-network-based functional connectivity (Least-Cost Path) will significantly increase the estimated effect size of environmental covariates (habitat selection) for otters by correctly attributing historical colonization lags to landscape resistance rather than environmental suitability.
expected_metric: Widely Applicable Information Criterion (WAIC)
success_threshold: A reduction in WAIC of >10 units and a statistically significant change (non-overlapping 95% CI) in the 'cropland' or 'riparian' coefficient estimates compared to the Euclidean baseline.
failure_condition: The LCP model fails to converge or shows no improvement in WAIC, suggesting Euclidean distance is a sufficient proxy for dispersal in this specific landscape.
first_24h_experiment: Download the Corine Land Cover and French river network (BD Carthage). Generate a 5km x 5km grid resistance surface where river pixels have low resistance and non-river pixels have high resistance. Compute a sparse LCP distance matrix for the first 1000 sites.
specific_intervention: Substitute the Euclidean distance calculation in the 'dispersal-pressure' kernel with a Least-Cost Path distance calculated over a resistance surface.
baseline: The paper's original Euclidean distance-based dynamic occupancy model.
metric: Posterior mean and 95% Credible Intervals of environmental coefficients (alpha_xi) and model deviance.
story_angle: Moving beyond 'as the crow flies'—how accounting for river networks reveals the true habitat needs of recovering species.

candidate_problem: Inability to model nucleus-to-nucleus variability in gene program co-activation structure
evidence_spans: the model aggregates attention over nuclei within each condition and cell type, which stabilizes the P × P estimate but precludes modeling nucleus-to-nucleus variability in co-activation.

Disease-associated heterogeneity within an annotated cell type is therefore not directly observable, and a hierarchical extension learning per-nucleus attention distributions alongside population means is a natural next step.
problem_evidence_spans: the model aggregates attention over nuclei within each condition and cell type, which stabilizes the P × P estimate but precludes modeling nucleus-to-nucleus variability in co-activation.
feasibility_evidence_spans: Applied to 191,890 prefrontal cortex nuclei from an AD atlas (Morabito et al., 2021 ) across three independent pathway vocabularies, ORBIT recovers biologically interpretable co-activation structure
ORBIT’s framework is vocabulary- and architecture-agnostic, and the intervention-consistent objective applies wherever features can be partitioned into meaningful groups
auto_research_experiment: Modify the ORBIT architecture to output a per-cell attention matrix A_i by conditioning the transformer on a cell-specific latent embedding. Train using the existing intervention-consistent loss but applied at the single-cell level, and evaluate if the variance in attention weights correlates with disease severity (Braak stage) within the same cell type.
available_data_or_benchmark: Morabito et al. (2021) AD snRNA-seq atlas (191,890 nuclei).
public_training_or_eval_data: https://www.synapse.org/#!Synapse:syn25808537
scientific_hypothesis: A hierarchical ORBIT model that learns per-nucleus attention distributions will identify sub-populations of cells within a single cell-type (e.g., Microglia) that exhibit 'pre-rewiring' signatures before global cell-type-level changes are detectable.
expected_metric: Variance of attention weights (Var(A)) across nuclei within a cell type.
success_threshold: The hierarchical model identifies a sub-cluster of nuclei with significantly different attention weights (p < 0.01, permutation test) that correlates with individual patient pathology scores better than the cell-type mean.
failure_condition: Per-nucleus attention weights are dominated by technical noise (dropout) and fail to show stable Jaccard stability (threshold < 0.50) across subsamples.
first_24h_experiment: Implement a Variational Autoencoder (VAE) bottleneck to generate cell-specific conditioning vectors for the ORBIT attention block and run on a subset (10,000 nuclei) of the Morabito dataset.
specific_intervention: Replace the global attention matrix A with a dynamic attention mechanism A(h_i) where h_i is a latent representation of cell i.
baseline: The original ORBIT model (cell-type aggregated attention).
metric: Correlation between per-nucleus attention variance and patient-level amyloid plaque density.
story_angle: Moving from 'average' cell behavior to identifying the specific 'renegade' cells that drive disease rewiring in the brain.

candidate_problem: The Hybrid-Lift model omits country-specific terms in life expectancy reconstruction, potentially understating cross-country divergence.
evidence_spans: omitting the explicit country-specific terms b_{x,i}k_{t,i}
This simplification may understate the true cross-country divergence in scenarios where specific factors exhibit strong persistent trends
problem_evidence_spans: This simplification may understate the true cross-country divergence in scenarios where specific factors exhibit strong persistent trends, and partially limits the claim that country-specific dynamics are fully exploited
feasibility_evidence_spans: The complete codebase, including data processing, model training, and validation notebooks, is publicly available.
auto_research_experiment: Re-introduce the country-specific factor (k_{t,i}) into the reconstruction formula using a secondary LSTM layer to model the residuals of the common factor.
available_data_or_benchmark: Human Mortality Database (HMD) for the 6-nation frontier cluster.
public_training_or_eval_data: https://github.com/davide-rindori/Actuarial-DS-Portfolio/tree/main/04_Multi_Population_Longevity_XAI
scientific_hypothesis: Modeling country-specific residuals with a secondary LSTM will capture the 'unit root' behavior in Sweden and West Germany more accurately than the common-factor-only reconstruction.
expected_metric: Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) of reconstructed life expectancy (e0).
success_threshold: 5% reduction in RMSE for Sweden and West Germany compared to the common-factor baseline.
failure_condition: The inclusion of country-specific terms leads to 'integration drift' where long-term projections become biologically implausible (e.g., crossing mortality curves).
first_24h_experiment: Extract the residuals from the current Hybrid-Lift common factor and perform a unit root test (ADF) to confirm the signal remains uncaptured.
specific_intervention: Add a residual-tracking LSTM to the reconstruction pipeline.
baseline: Hybrid-Lift common-factor-only reconstruction.
story_angle: Beyond the Average: Capturing unique national mortality trends with hierarchical neural networks.

candidate_problem: Fixed mixture component specification in Zero-Inflated Beta Mixture (ZIBM) models for microbiome mediation analysis
evidence_spans: The number of mixture components need to be specified in advance, which may affect model performance, and computational burden may increase in high-dimensional microbiome settings.
Future work includes data-driven selection of mixture components.
problem_evidence_spans: The number of mixture components need to be specified in advance, which may affect model performance
feasibility_evidence_spans: The real data analysis demonstrates the utility of the proposed model for microbiome studies with sparse and compositional mediators.
Compared to the MarZIC method’s application of the Arthur et al. ( 2013 ) study, the ZIBM framework identified eight additional bacterial families
auto_research_experiment: Implement a Dirichlet Process Mixture Model (DPMM) extension to the ZIBM framework to allow for an infinite mixture of Beta distributions where the number of components is learned from the data via MCMC or Variational Inference. Compare this against the fixed-component ZIBM on the Arthur et al. (2013) CRC dataset.
available_data_or_benchmark: Arthur et al. (2013) Colorectal Cancer (CRC) microbiome dataset (16S rRNA sequencing).
public_training_or_eval_data: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/bioproject/PRJNA190557
scientific_hypothesis: A data-driven, non-parametric selection of mixture components (e.g., via Dirichlet Process) will yield lower Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC) scores and higher sensitivity in identifying causal mediators compared to a fixed 2-component ZIBM model.
expected_metric: Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC) and Number of Validated Causal Mediators.
success_threshold: The automated selection model achieves a lower BIC in >70% of taxa and identifies at least 2 additional biologically relevant mediators confirmed by literature.
failure_condition: The computational overhead of non-parametric estimation leads to non-convergence in high-dimensional settings (taxa > 500) or the model collapses to a single component for all taxa.
first_24h_experiment: Download the Arthur et al. (2013) OTU table, implement a basic Beta Mixture Model with a GIBBS sampler for component selection, and test it on the top 10 most abundant taxa.
specific_intervention: Replace the fixed-component likelihood in the ZIBM framework with a Dirichlet Process Beta Mixture likelihood.
baseline: The fixed-component ZIBM model described in the paper.
story_angle: Moving beyond rigid statistical assumptions in microbiome research by allowing the data itself to dictate the complexity of microbial interactions.

candidate_problem: Overestimation of LLM performance in adaptive benchmarking due to selection bias (Winner's Curse).
evidence_spans: Correcting the Winner's Curse in Adaptive Benchmarking
problem_evidence_spans: Correcting the Winner's Curse in Adaptive Benchmarking
feasibility_evidence_spans: Towards Reliable LLM Evaluation
auto_research_experiment: Implement a bootstrap-based correction or a James-Stein estimator to adjust the leaderboard scores of top-performing models on MMLU and GSM8K.
available_data_or_benchmark: MMLU (Massive Multitask Language Understanding)
public_training_or_eval_data: https://github.com/hendrycks/test
scientific_hypothesis: Applying a shrinkage estimator to adaptive benchmarking results will reduce the gap between reported leaderboard performance and true out-of-distribution generalization.
expected_metric: Mean Absolute Error (MAE) between predicted and actual performance on a held-out test set.
success_threshold: A reduction in MAE by at least 15% compared to uncorrected 'best-of-N' reporting.
failure_condition: The correction leads to underestimation of performance or does not significantly differ from the raw maximum in low-variance settings.
first_24h_experiment: Download MMLU results for 10 open-source models, simulate 'adaptive selection' by picking the best of 5 prompts, and calculate the bias relative to the average prompt performance.
specific_intervention: Apply a statistical correction factor (e.g., empirical Bayes shrinkage) to the top-performing model scores.
baseline: Raw maximum score (Winner's Curse baseline).
story_angle: As LLMs compete for the top spot on leaderboards, we are likely measuring noise rather than progress; this research provides the 'statistical glasses' to see the true winners.
